commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a public `openclaw/plugin-sdk/json-rpc-plugin` entrypoint, docs, package export, generated SDK metadata, and tests for dispatching plugin tool, hook, HTTP route, and Gateway method calls to a stdio JSON-RPC child process.

PR surface: Source +651, Tests +584, Docs +112, Config +4, Generated 0, Other +4. Total +1355 across 12 files.

Reproducibility: yes. for the review findings: source inspection on the PR head shows raw `spawn()` and best-effort `child.kill()` in the new public child-process bridge. I did not run a live Windows wrapper-command or stubborn-child reproduction in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] The remaining blockers are concrete mechanical fixes in the new SDK helper and tests, but the PR is draft and maintainer-labeled so deterministic repair would still need maintainer opt-in.

target.title: feat: add JSON-RPC plugin bindings
target.body:
Closes #98004

## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw plugin authors who want to implement runtime logic in Rust, Python, Go, or another non-TypeScript language currently need to handroll process bridges behind each plugin surface. That duplicates stdio framing, lifecycle cleanup, timeout handling, environment inheritance policy, HTTP body handling, and Gateway response mapping across plugins.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds a public `openclaw/plugin-sdk/json-rpc-plugin` entrypoint with `defineJsonRpcPluginEntry`. The TypeScript entry remains the synchronous source of truth for OpenClaw plugin descriptors, while invoked tool, hook, HTTP route, and Gateway method calls dispatch to a lazily started child process over newline-framed JSON-RPC 2.0 on stdio. The implementation keeps existing OpenClaw auth/scope gates at the registered host surface and avoids introducing a socket listener.

## User Impact

Plugin authors can now build language-agnostic plugin runtimes while still using OpenClaw's existing plugin architecture and manifest model. Rust or other native runtimes only need a small JavaScript descriptor file plus a JSON-RPC stdio server.

AI-assisted: yes. I understand the code change and validated the touched SDK boundary.

## Evidence

- `pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check`
- `pnpm check:docs`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/plugin-sdk/json-rpc-plugin.ts src/plugin-sdk/json-rpc-plugin.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/json-rpc-plugin.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings

Earlier full SDK proof on this branch also passed:

- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:dts`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:check-exports`
- `pnpm docs:list`

Implementation size: 12 files changed, +1362/-7 total. Public runtime code is one new SDK helper (`src/plugin-sdk/json-rpc-plugin.ts`, 650 LOC) plus focused tests (`src/plugin-sdk/json-rpc-plugin.test.ts`, 585 LOC); the rest is export metadata, API/surface/docs generated metadata, and docs.
target.author: romneyda
